data.title: Replimune: Multiple Shots On Goal But A High Risk Binary Approaches
data.description:
Replimune (REPL) has two oncolytic virus therapies in clinical development, RP1 and RP2, with RP1 already under review for potential marketing approval in the US. The company previously received a complete response letter (CRL) when it submitted RP1 for approval in melanoma, but soon resubmitted its application, which could be approved or rejected come April 10, 2026. Beyond that, RP1 is in additional trials, as is RP2, which provides the company with multiple shots on goal. This article takes a look at catalysts ahead for REPL, and the recent financial update.

RP1 under review again

In July 2025, REPL announced it had received a CRL for its biologics license application (BLA) for RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec) plus nivolumab in advanced melanoma patients who were PD-1 experienced. The FDA at that time didn't seem happy with the IGNYTE trial, despite its 32.9% objective response rate, median duration of response of 33.7 months, and 2-year survival of 63.3%. For example, the FDA noted the trial couldn't be interpreted due to heterogeneity of the patient population. Following a type A meeting in September, the company resubmitted its BLA in October, with that submission being accepted and considered to be a complete response to the CRL.

During the past few months, Replimune has been working to address agency feedback. Additional information, data and analyses were included in the resubmission which will be part of the BLA review. The FDA indicated this resubmission is considered to be a complete response to the complete response letter received in July 2025.

October 20, 2025, press release from REPL.

The current Prescription Drug User Fee Act (PDUFA) target action date for the BLA is April 10, 2026. It isn't clear if the FDA will approve the BLA this time around. At the recent JP Morgan Healthcare Conference, the analyst asked if there had been new staff members involved in the review of REPL's BLA. Some new staff involved wouldn't necessarily mean that another CRL is incoming, but it could be viewed as creating additional uncertainty.

Anupam Rama, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division

Okay. And I just want to kind of confirm that your review team kind of at the oncology division, CBER, that's not seen meaningful turnover in terms of team leads and things of that nature?

Emily Hill, Chief Financial Officer, REPL

It's hard to know because as I stated, we don't have interactions and meetings where you can see everyone around the table. We do believe there are some new faces based on the follow-up questions from our prior BLA, but we know our project manager has remained the same.

Sushil Patel, CEO & Director, REPL

Yes. Just so we know in the Type A meeting, we did have some new attendees, new people, including OCE representatives at that meeting, and we believe they're probably now also involved in the ongoing review and information requests that we're getting.

JP Morgan Healthcare Conference, January 2026.

REPL does have other shots on goal with RP1 if the FDA remains unsatisfied. For example, RP1 can be used as monotherapy in patients where an anti-PD-1 is not appropriate, such as those who have had an organ transplant. For example, REPL's ARTACUS trial looks at RP1 monotherapy in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC), with recent data including a 34.6% objective response rate (9/36 patients) in locally advanced CSCC. As such, in a hypothetical situation where REPL's BLA is rejected again, the company can still continue developing RP1 in other indications while it considers its options of submitting again right away, or waiting for the confirmatory IGNYTE-3 trial to reach an interim.

Regarding IGNYTE-3, that trial compares RP1 plus nivolumab to physicians choice in melanoma patients who have had an anti-PD-1 and an anti-CTLA4 (if eligible). The current timeline, however, only has an interim analysis set for H2'27.

REPL notes that enrollment in the trial wasn't adversely impacted by the CRL, and so I think the company has a good chance of remaining on track with that trial.

RP2 is also in the clinic

As for RP2, the company's other oncolytic virus therapy in the clinic, the company notes the REVEAL study in metastatic uveal melanoma has already enrolled 50 patients (slide 27, January presentation). Although the planned enrollment in REVEAL is 280 patients, the company is still expecting to report data from 90 patients in Q1'27, which actually puts a readout from that trial ahead of the IGNYTE-3 interim.

Beyond REVEAL, the company is also enrolling patients in a trial of RP2 with atezolizumab/bevacizumab in hepatocellular carcinoma, and RP2 with durvalumab in biliary tract cancer. Preliminary data from the hepatocellular carcinoma cohort are expected in Q4'26, which provides a 2026 clinical readout for the company in a non skin-cancer indication.

Financial Overview

REPL finished their fiscal Q3'26 (which ended on December 31, 2025) with $269.1M in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. For Q3'26, REPL reported R&D expenses of $53.1M, SG&A expenses of $18.7M, and a net loss of $70.9M. REPL used its at-the-market sales agreement to raise an additional $20.8M in January 2026, which should also be considered. Further, in January 2026, REPL made a third amendment to its Hercules loan agreement that saw it draw a further $35M and move repayment of the debt (principal balance now approximately $82M) into 2027 instead of 2026. As a result, we might consider pro forma cash to be $324.9M and long-term debt to be ~$82M.

REPL's net cash used in operating activities was $224.2M for the nine-month period ending December 31, 2025, meaning at the current rate REPL would have runway for about 13 months, or until Q1'27. Indeed, REPL believes it has extended its runway into late Q1'27. The above calculation doesn't consider the company's debt, but that isn't due until the maturity date of October 1, 2027. Further, if REPL receives FDA approval prior to that date, the amortization and maturity date on its Hercules loan shall instead be October 1, 2029. Also, REPL could continue to use its at-the-market program to raise additional funds prior to the PDUFA date of April 10. REPL had $89.3M left under the ATM program at the end of 2025, and subtracting the $20.8M raised in January 2026, there would be approximately ~$68M worth of stock sales remaining. REPL might try to further boost its cash balance heading into the PDUFA date, to be prepared for the potential scenario where the company receives another CRL. Another $68M would cover about 80% of the company's long-term debt, so it would certainly help REPL prepare for the worst.

REPL trades with a market cap of $664M ($8.04 per share).

Risks

Although this article isn't advocating for a long position in REPL, given the hold rating, a few risks of a long position will be considered here.

Firstly, REPL could receive another complete response letter. The fact that the FDA soon accepted a resubmission of the company's BLA doesn't guarantee approval. If REPL's BLA is rejected again, the fall in the stock could be particularly pronounced, especially since concerns might arise about the company's ability to run its trials and repay its debt.

Further, REPL traded down about 20% in January, part of which could relate to the company using its ATM program to raise cash. If the company continues to use the program heading towards the April 10, 2026, PDUFA goal date, then the stock could trade down rather than run up into that catalyst.

Lastly, REPL could report delays or provide disappointing updates on enrollment rates or results from its trials. IGNYTE-3 had already enrolled over 60 patients out of a planned 400 at the time of the JPMorgan Healthcare Conference in mid-January, but if enrollment lags, the stock could fall.

Conclusions

REPL has catalysts ahead outside of its upcoming PDUFA date in April. Initial data from a trial of RP2 with atezolizumab/bevacizumab in hepatocellular Q4'26 could demonstrate the value of the company's oncolytic therapy outside of skin cancer. The company also has plenty of shots on goal in various skin cancers with RP1 both as monotherapy and as a combination, although a readout from the confirmatory trial IGNYTE-3 might not arrive until H2'27 or later. I believe the company produced compelling data from the IGNYTE study, and I hope the company has addressed the FDA's concerns with its resubmission, but I find it hard to bet on, and the company only has about 13 months of cash runway. I also believe the company will likely use its ATM program near-term to raise funds in case its BLA is not approved, which could hurt the stock near-term. I don't view the company as a good short, however, because the BLA could be approved early, for all we know, and trying to short what could be a modest pullback due to ATM usage might be high risk, low reward. Considering these factors, I currently rate REPL a hold.

data.title: Western Midstream: Still A High Yielder But No Longer A High Grower (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Slowing Growth

Western Midstream Partners, LP (WES) partnership units have had an impressive last 5 years, justifying the Buy rating I have maintained for most of that time frame. From lows below $3 during the 2020 crash, unit prices have improved to above $40. The quarterly distribution, which was cut in half to $0.311, has nearly tripled to $0.91 in 2025. Total return over the past 5 years has been over 290%, far exceeding the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) or the S&P 500 index fund (SPY).

WES first got its balance sheet in order, paring down leverage and funding organic growth out of free cash flow. Over time, WES also added some targeted acquisitions, such as Meritage Midstream Partners in Wyoming's Powder River Basin for cash in 2023. More recently, WES did a bigger M&A deal, adding to its water handling business with the purchase of Aris Water Solutions for a mix of cash and stock. WES was later able to offset some of this equity issuance by redeeming some of its sponsor Occidental's (OXY) units in exchange for switching to a fixed-fee contract from a cost-of-service model. This transaction took OXY's ownership of WES down to 39.5% of the limited partner units. This increased M&A activity could be a sign of slowing organic growth ahead.

In 2025, natural gas throughput was still up by 4%, thanks to 9% gains in the Delaware Basin, offset by declines elsewhere. WES is still investing in gas processing capacity in the Delaware, with its North Loving Phase 1 starting up in 2025 and Phase 2 under construction with a completion date of Q1 2027. Crude and Natural Gas Liquids (NGLs) throughput was up only 1% for the partnership, with 6% gains in the Delaware and declines elsewhere. Water throughput grew 7% organically but 40% overall, including just 2.5 months contribution from Aris.

For 2026, WES is more conservative on growth assumptions, predicting flat throughput on natural gas, a low to mid-single digit decrease in crude and NGLs, and an 80% increase in water due to Aris. On the earnings call, management signaled a pullback in upstream activity:

based on recent discussions with our producing customers and taking into account their updated forecast, it has become clear that many of our producers will reduce previously expected activity levels on acreage that we service, including portions of the Delaware Basin.

Financially, the partnership is predicting EBITDA of $2.5 - $2.7 billion, a 4.8% increase at the midpoint, but distribution growth of only 2.2%, to $0.93 quarterly.

While that is still an attractive 9% yield, there are some warning signals we need to look for to make sure it is sustainable. First, it is important that WES not cut corners on maintenance and safety to reduce costs. Second, we want to make sure WES maintains its capital discipline and does not overleverage itself again. Finally, the partnership should only pursue accretive M&A and avoid deals that grow the company without increasing earnings per unit. If WES can avoid these risky behaviors, the 9% yield looks safe even though growth is slowing.

Safety First, Capital Discipline A Close Second

WES has already cut operating costs in 2025. Some of this involves optimizing rental equipment, contract workers, and supply chain sourcing; however, they also mention "Asset maintenance programs and schedules".

With any energy company, cutting maintenance could raise the risk of injuries or releases in the future. I am not saying it automatically does or that WES is doing anything less safe. With proper Management of Change processes to consider and avoid what could go wrong, safe cost cutting is possible. Still, investors should be on the lookout for signs of declining safety performance. WES does not regularly discuss these metrics in quarterly releases, but some are included in the 10-K, where they make up part of the formula to determine executive bonuses.

For 2025, the Total Recordable Injury Rate was 0.52, significantly exceeding the target of 0.38.

(TRIR includes injuries or illnesses that result in any of the following: days away from work, restricted work or transfer to another job, medical treatment beyond first aid, loss of consciousness, or death.)

Fortunately, the Serious Injury Frequency (fatality or permanent, life-altering impairment) was zero in 2025, but it is important to understand and fix the root causes of the less severe injuries before they result in more serious ones.

On the positive side, system operability was 99.4%, exceeding the 98% target, and Release Intensity (volumes released divided by throughput) was less than half of the target.

One yellow flag on capital discipline is the return of the Distributable Cash Flow metric, as opposed to Free Cash Flow, which has been the main focus since 2020. DCF is basically operating cash flow minus maintenance capex, but not growth capex. The partnership could fund growth capex by issuing stock or debt without much of an impact on DCF. This is not an immediate problem, as WES has pared down its net debt/EBITDA leverage to around 3, compared to above 5 in 2019. The partnership currently has an investment grade debt rating. Also, under the new DCF target, distribution coverage ratio is about 1.3, so the distribution is adequately covered.

With manageable debt levels and an investment-grade rating, it is reasonable to relax capital discipline a little to pursue high return projects or acquisitions, but with greater flexibility to do deals, the risk of overpaying or overestimating the synergies also increases.

This risk was highlighted with rumors of WES looking to buy Kinetik Holdings (KNTK). At a $7.2 billion market cap plus $4 billion of debt, Kinetik's enterprise value is nearly half that of WES. Any deal would probably involve considerable unit issuance (diluting existing holders) or added debt (resulting in higher leverage and interest expense).

Kinetik has gathering and processing facilities both north and south of WES's main footprint in the Delaware Basin as well as shares in long-haul pipelines to natural gas hubs in East Texas.

Clearly some synergies could be achieved in a deal. Kinetik's ownership of a pipeline out of the Waha hub could also be a focus of future investment, as takeaway capacity limits have resulted in low prices at Waha, contributing to the upstream production cutbacks WES is now seeing with its current customers.

On the earnings call, the WES CEO stressed that WES would only deploy capital to sustain or grow the distribution but also stated an openness to bolt-on M&A with complementary assets and synergies. So, it does not sound like a Kinetik deal is fully ruled out but would have to be at the right price.

As I said in our -- in the prepared remarks, our strategy is unchanged, and that goes for the way we think about M&A. So again, our capital deployment strategy is clear and it's tight. We only deploy capital, whether it's organic or inorganic to sustain or grow the distribution. We have demonstrated that discipline clearly last year. It is unchanged going forward. So I'm a little annoyed that people seem to be questioning that a little bit by virtue of what's happening in the marketplace.

But second, the way we think about M&A, again, is really our preference is bolt-on M&A where we have opportunities for synergies. So it fits with our assets, our geographies. We have some way a reason and competency for owning the asset. So that's unchanged. And especially as a relatively new CEO, I guess I'm kind of popular. We have had -- I've met every CEO, private and public in this space. I'm pretty sure I haven't missed anybody.

Earnings Model

Going forward, I am assuming 4% EBITDA growth and $0.02 quarterly distribution growth each year through 2029. This allows for $800 million of capex in 2027, growing by $25 million per year thereafter. Note that the 2027 capex estimate is a reduction from $925 million expected in 2026, with North Loving 2 and the Pathfinder water pipeline still under construction.

With these assumptions, the distributions are covered, but there is not a huge amount of free cash flow left over for M&A, buybacks, or faster distribution growth without dipping into the existing cash balance. Any major expansions or M&A would likely require debt or stock issuance. That is not necessarily bad. Companies like Enbridge (ENB) finance growth this way, although they always make sure new capacity is supported by firm volume commitments with take-or-pay contracts. Even so, Enbridge recently has demonstrated only slightly higher dividend growth than what WES is predicting going forward. Added interest expense or unit dilution takes a big bite out of the added EBITDA from these deals, resulting in less EPS accretion.

If WES sticks by its commitment to only do deals that grow the distribution per unit and do not overestimate the synergies, it will be fine, but distribution growth will probably remain low.

Conclusion

Organic growth of Western Midstream Partners has slowed considerably in 2026 as producers cut back activity in the face of lower prices. Looking forward, organic EBITDA growth looks limited to 4% and distribution growth a little over 2%. WES is looking at cost cuts to enhance income but must be careful to not compromise on safety. Inorganic growth through M&A can also be tempting but must be accretive to EPS, considering any unit dilution or added leverage and interest expense.

However, even with limited organic growth and no inorganic growth, the 9% yielding distribution looks adequately covered and able to go up by around 2% annually. This is fine for income investors but less attractive to investors looking for growth. As a result, I am changing my rating on WES to Hold.

data.title: Klarna: Buy The Dip In Oversold Territory As Fair Financing Takes Off
data.description:
If there is one trend that has shaped the stock market completely in 2026, it's investors' risk-off attitude toward most growth stocks. The S&P 500 has remained near last year's highs, but underneath major sector shifts are occurring, with investors dumping software and internet stocks and buying up energy and semiconductor names.

Companies that had short stints as highly anticipated IPOs, like Klarna (KLAR), are now in deeply oversold territory. Since going public last September at $40 per share, Klarna has lost two-thirds of its value. It's staggering to remember that in 2021, Klarna raised money as a private company at a >$45 billion valuation, and today sits at roughly 10% of its former heights. But while we certainly agree that hype fed into the stock's overvaluation, when we take a sober look at Klarna today, we find a business that is rapidly scaling, growing its profits, and likely oversold due to irrational fear.

I had been very cautious on Klarna immediately upon its IPO, but I upgraded the stock to a buy rating in December, when it was trading around $30 per share. Now, with shares of Klarna rapidly losing half of their value in the space of a month, we're facing heavily oversold conditions in Klarna that aren't commensurate with the company's actual fundamental performance. We can now substantiate Klarna's valuation purely from its bottom line. With this in mind, I'm reiterating my buy opinion on the stock.

Klarna is approaching bargain stock territory

First, let's discuss the elephant in the room that knocked the stock sharply down post-earnings: the perception of a weak outlook for 2026. While we do agree that Klarna's implied slowdown in growth rates is a concern, we also have to take into account not only its own scale (making deceleration very likely), but the fact that Klarna's sinking valuation already prices in substantial risk.

As shown in the chart above, Klarna is guiding to >$155 billion in GMV. Wall Street had been hoping for $158.9 billion in GMV, so that was strike one against the company. Q1 GMV guidance also has GMV decelerating to 26-30% y/y growth, versus 32% y/y growth in Q4. We should note, however, that Klarna is guiding to a minimum of $155 billion in GMV. The company is likely baking in conservatism from a shaky global macro environment (which the recent Supreme Court strikedown of U.S. tariffs may help with). It may not be long until Klarna showcases above-guidance performance (as it did in Q4) and boosts its outlook beyond the ~$159 billion that Wall Street had hoped for anyway.

The company is also guiding to a minimum revenue take rate of 2.80%. Again using the company's minimum guidance statements here, full-year revenue would clock in at $4.34 billion (against $155 billion in GMV, and representing 24% y/y growth). An adjusted operating margin of >6.9% also means a minimum of $299.5 million in adjusted profits.

That's a sizable profit threshold, especially relative to Klarna's sinking valuation. At current post-earnings share prices near $13, Klarna trades at a market cap of $4.95 billion. Netting off the $16.56 billion of cash, debt securities, and consumer receivables on Klarna's balance sheet against $15.10 billion in consumer deposits, debt and merchant payables gives us an enterprise value of $3.39 billion.

This is a multiple of just 11.3x EV/FY26 adjusted operating profits. And again, the adjusted operating profit projection is based on the company's minimum guidance thresholds. Barring any major macro shocks that cause sharp GMV deterioration or unexpected credit losses, we think Klarna has a great shot at outperforming its initial outlook: while investors have a chance to buy a onetime red-hot unicorn at a deeply discounted price.

Q4 download

Let's now go through Klarna's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Klarna's GMV grew 32% y/y to $38.70 billion. This is the core growth driver for the company: the more transactions that are processed through the Klarna network, the greater Klarna's chance to deploy its balance sheet in financing deals while reaping transaction processing fee income. As shown in the chart below, Klarna's GMV has seen a consistent pattern of acceleration, improving seven points from 25% yards/y growth in Q3. So while Q1 guidance (calling for 26-30% growth) certainly does represent deceleration from Q4, it's certainly not a bad result, and still at a stronger pace than every other quarter in FY25 barring Q4.

Where Klarna tripped up against Street estimates, however, was on transaction margin dollars (TMD). As a reminder, this is a custom metric for Klarna that is essentially the company's version of "gross margin." It subtracts transactional costs from Klarna's total revenue (from both transaction fees and interest income), but it importantly also includes an upfront charge for credit loss provisions (the estimated losses on the loan portfolio, expensed upfront upon origination).

This means that the better Klarna does on originations, the worse its TMD metric will be in the near term. That said, loans that are originated in Q4 will get the headwind of the credit provision in the same quarter, but will be generating interest income for several quarters over the life of that loan.

Klaras transaction margin dollars of $372 million in Q4 grew 17% y/y, which fell below the company's guidance of $390-$400 million. Importantly, this wasn't due to a lack of GMV or revenue take rates, both of which beat the company's estimates, but due to far stronger adoption of Fair Financing lending deals in the U.S., which is a favorable catalyst for long-term interest income growth. Overall Fair Financing transactions grew 154% y/y to $4.50 billion in GMV (12% of the company's total, doubling from 6% in the year-ago quarter).

As such, we're not at all worried about the near-term TMD miss, as it's primarily driven by the company's conservative upfront provision accounting.

Meanwhile, we note that the company is achieving improving credit quality metrics in the U.S. market. As shown below, delinquency rates for loans that are both 30+ and 60+ days past due in the 2023/2024 loan vintages are all trending downward over time:

Also important for investors to note is that Klarna is deepening its digital banking footprint in Europe. As the company's Fair Financing deals grow, it's critical as well that Klarna is able to keep a pipeline of low-cost deposits to fund its loans, allowing the company to build a healthy net interest income spread. The company's total number of banking customers doubled y/y in Q4, while total deposits grew 37% y/y to $13.0 billion:

Risks and key takeaways

In my view, much of Klarna's near-term losses are due to fear, as well as a lack of understanding about why the company is missing transaction margin dollar estimates (driven by the success of Fair Financing deals).

Of course, there are risks that we need to keep in mind here. The global macroeconomy remains shaky, and a near-term recession would hit both GMV as well as increase loan losses. That said, when Klarna is trading at ~11x forward operating income (using the conservative minimum thresholds for the company's FY26 guidance), it's hard not to acknowledge that the risk is already fully priced in.

Take advantage of oversold conditions to buy this stock.

data.title: Comstock Resources: Attempting To Rebuild Production With Increased Spending
data.description:
Comstock Resources (CRK) is going more aggressive with its spending plans in 2026 in order to rebuild some of its production. It reported a $220 million free cash flow deficit from operations in 2025 while its production declined by approximately 15% year-over-year.

I am modeling Comstock's 2026 production growth at 5%, while it is projected to have $463 million in cash burn at current strip prices.

Despite that cash burn, I am increasing Comstock's estimated value from $13.75 to $14.50 per share to account for some expected progress in reducing its Western Haynesville D&C costs.

While there are a large amount of natural gas resources in the Western Haynesville, the value of Comstock's position there depends on its ability to improve the economics of Western Haynesville wells. Comstock is targeting a $300 per lateral foot reduction in Western Haynesville drilling costs, which is a step towards making the Western Haynesville economically competitive with legacy Haynesville wells.

2025 Results

Comstock's 2025 production results were relatively mediocre, as it fell short of its initial guidance. Comstock initially expected 1,300 to 1,400 MMCFE per day in 2025 production with $1.0 billion to $1.1 billion in D&C costs. It finished 2025 with 1,234 MMCFE per day in average production with $1.055 billion in exploration and development capex, including $1.008 billion in exploration and development D&C costs. Comstock's divestitures had a minimal effect on its 2025 production, and without those divestitures, I estimate it would have ended up with approximately 1,238 MMCFE per day in production.

Comstock's operating costs were in-line with expectations for the year, with the exception of production and other taxes, which ended up at $0.09 per Mcfe. This was significantly better than its guidance of $0.14 to $0.18 per Mcfe.

Overall, though, Comstock ended up with a $220 million free cash flow deficit from operations in 2025 despite a roughly 15% decline in production compared to 2024.

Notes On The Western Haynesville

Comstock is continuing to work on proving out its Western Haynesville acreage and attempting to lower D&C costs there. It is currently operating four rigs in the Western Haynesville out of its nine total drilling rigs.

Comstock's Western Haynesville wells have delivered excellent production results so far, but despite some progress with costs over time, the Western Haynesville economics still appear to be weaker than legacy Haynesville wells.

Comstock appears to have reduced the cost of Western Haynesville two-mile laterals down to around $32 million, but getting it down to around $25 million may be necessary for the Western Haynesville to be fully economically competitive with legacy Haynesville wells.

Comstock believes that the Western Haynesville could have a tremendous amount of recoverable reserves, which it estimates at close to 50 Tcf net to its working interest. I also believe that there is a very large amount of natural gas there, but my opinion continues to be that the value of that inventory is highly dependent on the ability to lower D&C costs to make it more competitive with the legacy Haynesville.

As well, Comstock's exploration activities have mainly focused on a relatively small part of the Western Haynesville so far, so it will need to continue derisking other parts of its acreage.

Comstock is also planning on redeeming the preferred units held by Quantum in its Pinnacle Gas Services midstream subsidiary. Quantum invested $300 million to fund the midstream build out and was entitled to a 12% dividend on invested capital and 80% of any distributions until certain return hurdles were met (and then it would end up with 30% ownership). Comstock is paying $440 million (not including accrued and unpaid distributions) to redeem Quantum's preferred units. Comstock expects to sell common equity in Pinnacle to pay for that redemption and believes that Pinnacle should be largely self-funding going forward.

Potential 2026 Outlook

I had previously modeled Comstock's 2026 production at 1,200 MMCFE per day, with $1.15 billion in capex. Comstock is being more aggressive in terms of rebuilding its production and expects 1,250 to 1,400 MMCFE per day in 2026 production, with $1.4 billion to $1.5 billion in capex.

I am thus updating my model of Comstock's 2026 production to put it at 1,300 MMCFE per day. I am assuming that Comstock's production ends up slightly under its guidance midpoint, as its production ended up below its quarterly guidance midpoint for every quarter in 2025.

Comstock's Q1 2026 production is expected to be relatively low due to the schedule of wells being turned-in-line. Comstock expects substantial production growth during the latter half of the year.

At current strip of $3.90 NYMEX natural gas for 2026, I project Comstock to end up with $1.62 billion in revenues after hedges.

The February 2026 contract for NYMEX natural gas settled at $7.46, but prices for March 2026 to June 2026 are currently averaging around $3.07.

Projected Debt Position

Comstock ended 2025 with $2.825 billion in net debt, including $260 million in credit facility debt, and with $24 million in cash on hand.

Comstock's projected cash burn would increase its net debt to $3.288 billion at the end of 2026, and it may have roughly $700 million in credit facility debt at the end of the year.

Comstock's leverage would be around 2.7x at the end of 2026 in this scenario, close to the 2.6x leverage it reported at the end of 2025.

This is a relatively high amount of leverage for an upstream company, but Comstock does have the safety net of the Jones family. Comstock's bankruptcy risk is thus quite low, but there is some risk of dilution if its net debt and leverage increase too much.

Notes On Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Comstock's value from $13.75 per share to $14.50 per share based on $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas after 2026. This increase assumes that there is a high likelihood that Comstock achieves its target of reducing its Western Haynesville drilling costs by around $300 per lateral foot. This would bring its Western Haynesville costs down to around $29 million for a two-mile lateral.

At $14.50 per share, Comstock's enterprise value would be a bit over $7.5 billion with its projected net debt at the end of 2026. I'd attribute around $2 billion of that value to the undeveloped locations in the Western Haynesville. That $2 billion does not include the value of its producing wells there.

Comstock reported $3.06 billion in PD PV-10 at $3.387 Henry Hub natural gas. At my long-term commodity price of $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas instead, I estimate Comstock's PD PV-10 at around $3.6 billion. This is based on its year-end 2025 reserves. Comstock's 2026 development may be able to push that to $4+ billion by the end of the year.

I remain comfortable with that $3.75 long-term natural gas price. The current strip for the next couple of years is averaging slightly below that (with 2027 and 2028 averaging $3.70), but this has also ranged slightly higher than $3.75 in the recent past as well.

I should note that Jerry Jones has paid an average of approximately $7.25 per share for his stake in Comstock, so my $14.50 valuation estimate is double that. My valuation estimate is also around 26% higher than the highest price that Jones has been willing to purchase Comstock shares at so far.

Conclusion

While 2026 natural gas strip prices have improved since I last looked at Comstock, its projected cash burn has also increased due to its plans to build back up its production levels. I now project Comstock to end up with $463 million in cash burn in 2026.

That cash burn isn't a major issue yet, but it is something to keep an eye on. Comstock believes that it can reduce Western Haynesville drilling costs by $300 per lateral foot, which would improve its economics there.

While it still has more work to do after that, I have increased my estimate of Comstock's value to $14.50 per share based on the assumption that it should be able to achieve those savings.

data.title: FDLO: A Short-Term Harbor, Yet Beware Of Long-Term Underperformance
data.description:
With the S&P 500 having a not particularly dire yet clearly quite lackluster start to the year, some investors might be considering taking cover from the ripple effects of the AI moment of reckoning, which has been reverberating not just through high-priced software recently, in a style factor that promises them at least some relief for exhausted nerves—low volatility. And here, a few ETFs, including the Fidelity Low Volatility Factor ETF (FDLO), a passively managed vehicle beating the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year, spring to mind.

However, while the AI dilemma (or dilemmas) induced volatility is the problem, and low beta-focused portfolios might look like a relatively inexpensive, quick, and promising solution, I would defend my regular stance on that factor: it gives some temporary relief, but longer-term side effects are meaningful returns missed during recoveries. So, I add FDLO to my low volatility ETFs coverage list with a Hold rating.

FDLO Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the Fidelity website, the Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Factor Index SM is the keystone of FDLO's strategy. As explained in the summary prospectus, this index

... is designed to reflect the performance of stocks of large and mid-capitalization U.S. companies with lower volatility than the broader market.

Regarding the methodology, its central ingredient is the 'composite score' that relies on the triad of measures:

The Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Factor Index SM is constructed using Fidelity's rules-based proprietary index methodology. Stocks are ranked within each sector and given a composite score based on three measures of volatility: low volatility of returns, low beta (a measure of market sensitivity), and low earnings volatility. Within each sector, composite scores are adjusted based on market capitalization. Stocks with the highest composite scores within each sector are identified for inclusion in the index.

What I should also highlight is that the index has a complex weighting scheme:

Each sector is neutral-weighted relative to the broader equity market. Within each sector, each stock is weighted based on its market capitalization in the broader equity market plus an overweight adjustment that is equal for all constituents within that sector.

The proposed merger with FULVX

Additionally, it is worth paying attention to the fact that "Fidelity is proposing to merge Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Equity Fund into" FDLO. As per the Q&A document, the anticipated merger closing date is "on or about 5/8/2026." Nevertheless, the nuance is that shareholder approval of the merger is necessary. Consequently,

If adjournment is required, dates will be updated accordingly.

I believe investors should read the prospectus and the Q&A document available on the Fidelity website for a deeper understanding of the process.

I should mention that the merger, if it proceeds, will not result in a substantial increase in FDLO's AUM. As of February 20, the AUM stood at $1.56 billion. And Fidelity U.S. Low Volatility Equity Fund's (FULVX) portfolio net assets were at $20.91 million as of January 31.

FDLO vs. SPLV and IVV: Portfolio Composition

What is inside the FDLO portfolio at this point, and how is it different from that of the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF?

While this might sound counterintuitive, I would argue that in terms of exposures, the FDLO portfolio, which encompassed 126 common stocks as of February 18 (other asset classes accounted for around 0.33% of it), is closer to IVV than, for instance, to the Invesco S&P 500® Low Volatility ETF (SPLV). And this can explain its past outperformance (before 2026) vs. a few low-volatility ETFs and its underperformance vs. the group this year.

First, let us look at the overlap.

Parameter FDLO IVV
Stocks present in both FDLO and IVV 97.96% 51.89%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

The fact is, nearly all FDLO's holdings are S&P 500 constituents, and in the IVV ETF, they account for the lion's share of its net assets. Of course, the overlap tells us nothing in terms of what stocks are over- or under-weighted; hence, it is impossible to hypothesize about the differences between their factor stories using this fact only, so we will be incorporating much more data shortly.

Next, looking at FDLO's overlap with SPLV, we see that less than 32% of its net assets are deployed to names that are present in the portfolio of the latter.

Parameter FDLO SPLV
Stocks present in both FDLO and SPLV 31.85% 56.82%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

And to give a bit more context, I should also mention that low-beta names that qualified for SPLV account for just about 18% of the IVV portfolio.

Parameter SPLV IVV
Stocks present in both SPLV and IVV 99.92% 18.08%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

Next, looking at the group of the key ten companies, we see that FDLO features seven trillion-dollar names together accounting for 29.8% of its portfolio (and IVV has exposure to all nine that exist at the moment, with 36.77% allocated). Oddly enough, FDLO is even overweight in all of them (but AMZN's weights in these portfolios are on par).

Company Ticker Weight in FDLO, as of February 19 Weight in IVV, as of February 19 Sector Market Cap, $, as of February 20
Apple Inc. (AAPL) 7.48% 6.56% Information Technology 3,825.61
Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) 5.43% 5.40% Communication 3,667.44
Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) 5.47% 5.04% Information Technology 2,958.82
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) 3.38% 3.39% Consumer Discretionary 2,199.16
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) 2.42% 2.39% Communication 1,631.01
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) 3.71% 2.69% Information Technology 1,583.54
Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) 1.91% 1.55% Financials 1,072.43
Walmart Inc. (WMT) 1.10% 0.93% Consumer Staples 995.23
Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) 1.87% 1.38% Health Care 914.10
JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) 1.82% 1.43% Financials 830.82

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha. For IVV, weights of GOOGL and GOOG were summed up. FDLO has no exposure to GOOG

And how many does SPLV feature? One, and it is Berkshire Hathaway, with a 1.02% weight.

So, in the FDLO portfolio, investors will not find a utilities-, consumer staples-heavy equity mix that they might expect from a strategy revolving around low beta. Instead, it is heavy in information technology and financials, and so is IVV.

Next, compared to SPLV, FDLO is underweight in utilities by 19.8% and overweight in IT by 28.43%.

Just to give a bit more color on that, compared to IVV, FDLO is underweight in four GICS sectors, and the median difference in allocations is -0.18%, as per my calculations. For the seven sectors it is overweight in, the median difference is 0.28%. But the story is quite different when its exposures are compared to SPLV's:

Parameter FDLO
Median difference in weights (sectors FDLO is overweight in vs. SPLV) 2.97%
Median difference in weights (sectors FDLO is underweight in vs. SPLV) -9.36%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha

Factor Exposures

So, we should not be surprised by the fact that in terms of factors, the FDLO portfolio is closer to IVV that to SPLV.

Yes, this is a portfolio with a tilt toward the low volatility factor, as it is quite clearly illustrated by the weighted average 24-month beta of 0.78. It also has better valuation characteristics than the IVV portfolio (and larger exposure to value is typically a by-product of low volatility strategies) and weaker growth. And all these facts have secured FDLO's outperformance vs. IVV this year, as investors have been rethinking the growth premia and rotating to less volatile, cheaper names.

However, the FDLO portfolio still has an over $1 trillion weighted average market cap, as per my calculations, quite close to that of the S&P 500 ETF but 8.9x larger than SPLV's.

Next, FDLO has a 24% lower Price/Sales ratio than IVV. But the SPLV portfolio is considerably cheaper, as its P/S is 2x lower, while its exposure to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher is 14.5%, not particularly impressive yet still 2.1x larger than that of the Fidelity ETF.

And as far as the beta coefficient is concerned, the SPLV portfolio's 24-month weighted average beta is only 0.3. This is because 0.92 beta of Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) is the highest in its basket. At the same time, 37.8% of FDLO's holdings have that coefficient above 1.

FDLO Performance: Better Than Peers Yet Still Mostly Bleak

FDLO is beating IVV this year by around 1.08% as of writing this article.

Still, in terms of exposures, it is closer to IVV rather than to SPLV, it is unsurprisingly lagging this vehicle meaningfully, as well as the State Street® SPDR® US Large Cap Low Volatility Index ETF (LGLV), which is currently overweight in industrials and underweight in IT, among others. However, FDLO is a little bit ahead of the iShares MSCI USA Min Vol Factor ETF (USMV), which, compared to SPLV, is overweight in IT and underweight in utilities (26.51% and 7.57% allocation, respectively).

Speaking about the long-term performance, we see a somewhat ambiguous story. On the one hand, FDLO's similarities with IVV secured its edge over SPLV and USMV, principally because of its larger exposure to the growth factor. So, since its inception in 2016, it has outperformed them solidly.

LGLV, which changed its benchmark in December 2016, has also trailed FDLO.

On the other hand, beating IVV was still well beyond FDLO's capabilities.

So, over the October 2016–January 2026 period, it beat USMV and SPLV both in terms of annualized and risk-adjusted returns (the latter measured using the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) but still delivered one essential disadvantage inherent to most low-volatility names.

The issue is its upside capture ratio of just 82.37%. So, while it did offer some downside protection (not as strong as in the cases of USMV and SPLV though), it still was unable to keep pace with IVV during recoveries.

Final Thoughts

The conclusion today does not deviate from the one I arrived at in the November article on LGLV. While I acknowledge the relative allure of the low beta in the current market environment, I am standing behind the view that exchanging longer-term upside for some tactical, short-term safety is not worth it.

Another issue I should highlight is that FDLO has material differences from SPLV and is closer to IVV than to this low volatility-focused peer when it comes to composition. This explains why it has outperformed SPLV before 2026 but has been lagging it this year. So, for investors who are aware of the low beta strategies' long-term disadvantages and are still willing to rotate to them at this point, SPLV looks like a better choice.

So, while FDLO is, of course, not a fundamentally bad vehicle, given its advantages over SPLV (i.e., larger exposure to the quality factor and outperformance in the past), as well as its relatively low expense ratio of 15 bps, I believe reasons that would persuade me to become a FDLO bull are absent.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - The Gavel Falls On Global Tariffs As Inflationary Fears Return To The Fold
data.description:
Week in review - Tariffs and inflation

  • The US Supreme Court struck down the administration's broad global tariffs under an emergency act (IEEPA). However, the administration is pivoting to alternative legal tools.
  • US financial markets reacted positively to the tariff ruling but had to contend with a disappointing 1.4% GDP report and higher-than-expected inflation data (PCE price index up 0.4% MoM).
  • A 5% weekly gain in oil prices, combined with persistent inflation data, has brought inflationary pressures back to the fore.
  • The market's attention shifts to high-stakes corporate earnings, especially Nvidia, as the bellwether for the AI boom and crucial economic data, including the Australian Monthly CPI and US Consumer Confidence.

When looking back at the week that was, there is no place better to start than the Supreme Court ruling in the United States.

In a significant legal blow to the administration, the US Supreme Court ruled on February 20, 2026, that President Trump exceeded his constitutional authority by using the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) to bypass Congress and impose broad global tariffs.

While the 6–3 decision effectively strikes down the legal justification for many of the administration's "Liberation Day" and fentanyl-related levies, it does not mean the end of trade restrictions.

The ruling specifically targets the use of emergency statutes for taxation, yet leaves intact several other tariffs, such as those on steel, aluminum, and certain auto parts that are grounded in different legal frameworks like Section 232 or Section 301.

Consequently, while the court has dismantled the specific "emergency" scaffolding the President relied upon, the administration has already signaled it will pivot to alternative statutory tools to keep its wider trade agenda standing.

What is the way forward?

The Supreme Court’s decision focused strictly on the boundaries of executive power rather than the merits of trade protectionism, meaning the administration's broader tariff objectives remain very much alive. While the previous legal justifications have been stripped away, they are being rapidly replaced by new statutory foundations, ushering in a volatile transition period for the global economy.

Businesses now face a period of deep instability as they navigate the unlikely prospect of receiving full refunds for past duties and the high probability of "replacement tariffs" that will restore costs to their previous levels.

Ultimately, while the specific legal "scaffolding" has been dismantled, the administration is already rebuilding its trade barriers; regardless of the court's stance on the Constitution, the era of high tariffs appears far from over.

How did markets react?

US financial markets reacted positively to the Supreme Court's decision on Friday, with all three major indexes climbing immediately following the ruling and securing a winning week overall.

While investors were encouraged by the legal blow to the administration’s trade barriers, they also had to weigh a disappointing GDP report showing growth slowing to 1.4% alongside higher-than-expected inflation data.

Treasury yields moved upward as the loss of tariff revenue sparked concerns over a widening fiscal deficit and increased bond supply.

Internationally, the optimism was even more pronounced; Europe’s STOXX 600 (STOXX) index surged to a new all-time high, and gold prices continued their ascent as a weakening dollar and persistent geopolitical uncertainty fueled demand for safe-haven assets.

The continued rise in Gold prices is a nod to the uncertainty which Friday's decision brings despite the optimism. In short, the decision in some ways brings more questions than answers.

Inflation back to the fore

The US PCE price index rose 0.4% month-over-month in December 2025, following a 0.2% increase in November, the most since February and above market expectations of 0.3%.

This, coupled with a 5% weekly gain for Oil prices, has reignited fears of inflationary pressures returning, which would complicate matters for the global economy.

In December 2025, the European Central Bank projected that a 14% spike in oil prices would potentially add 0.5 percentage points to eurozone inflation over the long term while shaving a modest 0.1 percentage points off annual growth.

This outlook is particularly sensitive to Europe’s heavy reliance on imported energy, which often causes the Euro to weaken against the Dollar as fuel costs rise. Since those projections were made, oil prices have already climbed by that exact 14% margin, bringing the ECB’s cautionary scenario into reality.

Meanwhile, across the Atlantic, the Federal Reserve expressed concern in its January 2026 meeting minutes about the persistent risk of inflation remaining above target, even suggesting that future interest rate hikes could be necessary.

Despite these hawkish signals from central bankers, investors appear relatively unfazed, continuing to price in two rate cuts for the year, a sentiment supported by the fact that domestic gasoline prices remain near multi-year lows, providing a crucial psychological buffer for the markets.

If oil prices do continue to rise and the geopolitical situation in the Middle East continues to escalate, there may be real risks to the inflationary outlook moving forward.

The Week Ahead

The week ahead sees the focus shift from macroeconomic policy debates to high-stakes corporate earnings and inflation data.

The AI Litmus Test: Nvidia and Software Earnings

The primary catalyst for Wall Street will be Nvidia’s (NVDA) quarterly report. As the bellwether for the artificial intelligence boom, Nvidia’s results and guidance will determine if the massive valuations in the semiconductor sector remain justified.

Beyond hardware, the spotlight moves to the "software layer" of AI. Results from Salesforce (CRM), Snowflake (SNOW), Intuit (INTU), Zoom (ZM), and Zscaler (ZS) will be scrutinized to see if enterprise spending on AI software is finally translating into significant revenue growth. These reports follow a period of market stagnation caused by hawkish Fed signals and rising geopolitical tensions.

Central Banks and the "balancing act" Dilemma

Central banks face a difficult balancing act, often described as a choice between fighting persistent inflation and supporting slowing growth.

United States: On Wednesday, the CB Consumer Confidence Index will be a vital indicator of whether high interest rates and inflation are finally breaking the American consumer’s resilience.

Australia: Wednesday also brings the Monthly CPI indicator. If inflation remains above the RBA’s target band, markets may price in a higher probability of a March rate hike.

Japan: The market will watch a speech by BoJ’s Takada on Thursday for hints regarding the timing of further policy normalization, alongside Friday's Industrial Production and Retail Sales data.

Energy and Geopolitics

Rising oil prices (with Brent Crude seeing recent volatility) remain a wildcard. Tensions between the US and Iran have added a risk premium to energy markets, complicating the inflation outlook for central banks. Investors will monitor whether energy costs continue to climb, potentially forcing a "higher for longer" interest rate environment.

Summary of Key Dates:

Feb 24 (Tue): US Factory Orders.

Feb 25 (Wed): Nvidia Earnings; AU Monthly CPI; US Consumer Confidence.

Feb 26 (Thu): Salesforce & Snowflake Earnings; BoJ Takada Speech.

Feb 27 (Fri): Japan Industrial Production; US Jobless Claims.

Market Sentiment: Expect a "show me the money" attitude from investors. If AI leaders fail to beat high expectations or if inflation prints hot in Australia and Europe, the current market divergence, where the ASX 200 (AS51) has outperformed lagging US indices, could widen.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

From a technical perspective, the US dollar index (DXY) has had an impressive week and rally, which finally ran out of steam around the 98.00 handle.

Without a daily candle close above the 98.00 handle, the overall bearish trend remains intact despite the strong rally.

If the DXY is able to break higher, there is a confluence level just above which houses the 100- and 200-day MA and rests around the 98.50 handle.

It will be an intriguing week for the US dollar as markets fully digest the Supreme Court tariff decision and the Trump administration's response.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart, February 20, 2026

data.title: Coupang: Data Breach Costs Two Years
data.description:
Introduction

Coupang (CPNG) was hit by a massive data breach affecting over 33 million customers, which the Korean regulator attributed to poor cybersecurity. The impact on the shares was quick, with a 50% drop from the recent high. The question on my mind is, do I sell or buy more? Thus, I updated the valuation matrix and decided to buy more. The data breach may cost CPNG substantial fines and operating costs, but it has not led to the extinction of its brand. The consensus has incorporated a drop in margin growth that delays absolute earnings levels by about two years.

Breach Impact

As amply reported in the media, the company suffered a massive data breach with more than 33 million customer accounts exposed. The regulators have found Coupang at fault and will probably impose fines and remedies that will hurt margins. As discussed in previous articles, CPNG was steadily increasing fees and gaining scale, which would lead to substantial improvements in monetizing its e-commerce platform in Korea. This will likely be delayed or reduced in magnitude.

The new consensus figures seem to incorporate this margin hit, with cash earnings margins (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation) growing 100 bp vs 200 bp previously, or from 5.4% to 4.6% in 2026. This has a nearly $400m cut to previously estimated cash earnings, which cuts growth from 70% in YE26 to 39%. By most metrics, this is still quite good growth and margin expansion. The risk to revenue seems muted with a modest estimated decline, and the most recent data see CPNG neck and neck with Naver (NHNCF).

4Q25 Results Preview

Since the data breach occurred in January 2026, the 4Q25 results should not be affected by reputational issues, customer discounting, etc. The market expects continued trends from the year, with Korean operations growing at around 10% in revenue and steady margin gains. The developing markets, including Taiwan, are a growth focus, and CPNG has sacrificed margins to gain market share. Post-breach, I would not expect the growth strategy to change, but the Korean market may suffer from attrition and higher costs, as outlined above in the new consensus estimates for full 2026.

Financial Estimates

The full impact of the breach is being incorporated into lower margins by the consensus, as discussed earlier. It’s still guesswork, as we don’t know how much the added cybersecurity will cost Coupang, while delayed fee increases and scale gains may offset much of the cost/margin loss from 2026 forward. The 2025 estimates also seem to factor in some 4Q25 weakness attributable to operations outside Korea. The new consensus continues to expect gradual margin gains and increasing earnings and FCF. The company is net cash, so any fines or restitution should not damage the balance sheet. Long term, by 2028, net income is still forecast to increase by more than 4x on under 7% EBITDA margins.

Valuation

Given the lower absolute earnings estimates for 2026-2028 and the growth rate, I reduce the YE26 price target to $35 from $52, maintaining the 1x PEG target. This is in line with the consensus $31 and 0.9x PEG or 31x P/CE (cash earnings). The company will need to deliver on these reduced estimates, rectify cybersecurity issues, and pay fines. I expect continued volatility through 2H26 as results and news flow normalize.

Peer Comps

As with most equity analysis, I compare the company to a broad peer group to measure growth rates and valuation metrics. The e-commerce sector seems discounted relative to medium-term cash earnings growth rates; most trade at under 0.7x PEG or 12x P/CE, which may be due to crowding out or rotation into the AI trade.

Risk

Coupang operates in a highly competitive market for a digitally astute customer base that has made monetization or margin gains difficult. This continued environment could reduce the expected margin and subsequent earnings growth. The data breach may cause reputational damage and force CPNG to discount customers or reduce traffic/revenue, which could cut into margins and the earnings outlook.

Conclusion

I rate CPNG a Buy. The company suffered a significant reputational hit that will most likely result in reduced margins and slower growth in 2026 and 2027. However, the share price decline appears to over-incorporate this new lower-earnings scenario, with a 100% upside and a risk-return balance worth the risk.

data.title: First Solar: Buy The Dip, Heavy Growth, Strong Demand, Strategic Moves
data.description:
First Solar Inc. (FSLR) had seen steady gains for nine months since the tariff announcements that shocked markets last April. Over the last 8 weeks or so, the stock has scaled back. Earnings are arriving in a few days to wrap up 2025 results, and this renewable semiconductor stock seems to be in an interesting position as demand for their product continues to rise. FSLR is a leading U.S.-based solar technology and energy solutions company primarily focused on the design, manufacture, and sale of advanced photovoltaic (PV) solar modules and large-scale solar power systems. They specialize in thin-film cadmium telluride (CdTe) solar panels, which offer a lower-carbon profile and competitive cost structure for utility-scale solar installations across domestic and international markets. They primarily sell to utilities, independent power producers, and commercial and industrial companies.

FSLR is recognized as the largest thin‑film solar panel producer in the world, with almost 50% of global market share. The stock is up about 48% over one year but has scaled back roughly 13% since the beginning of 2026. This pullback has pushed me to a buy rating for FSLR with Q4 earnings on the horizon. Outside of a slightly damper balance sheet adjustment, earnings continue to fly, profits and cash are strong, valuation is steady, and they are the global leader in a healthy, growing market.

Soaring Growth

Growth has been exceptional for FSLR. EPS has soared by a compounded annual growth rate of 44.2% over 5 years. They nearly doubled their sales YoY, reaching a record $1.594 billion last quarter. Analysts expect earnings next week to arrive at $5.22, almost a dollar higher than their already record-breaking prior quarter. In Q4 2022, they were unprofitable, posting $-0.07 EPS. This growth is phenomenal, and I suspect them to beat earnings again, as growth has been fueled by heightened demand for solar modules worldwide, and the firm remains strong in backlog. Last quarter they reported contracted backlog for future module sales of 53.7 GW, valued at 16.4 billion through 2030. The growth does not stop here for FSLR.

Healthy Margins

FSLR wasn’t consistently profitable until 2023, as capital expenditures continued to ramp up as management could see a clear path toward strong industry demand. Free cash flow margin has finally broken out as cash from operations exploded last quarter to $1.27 billion, from being negative in the same quarter last year. This has been a result of a flipped script on accounts receivable to the green, more module sales, and improved gross margins on each sale.

Gross margin last quarter was down YoY as management noted higher costs in some areas and lower tax credits; however, has still seen a great trend of margin results. Net income margin at 27.7% towers over the sector median of 5.4%. They continue to optimize their industry-leading cadmium‑telluride (CdTe) thin‑film modules through vertical integration by expanding manufacturing footprint and process innovation. I don’t even necessarily need to see more growth on margins; I just want to see them remain steady, with their already high market share and growing module sales.

Valuation Stable

After a stretch of unprofitability and inconsistency, FSLR now shows a maturing and more normalized valuation profile. Trailing PE is 18x, and forward is 16x, both lower than the sector median, and as you can see by the chart above, extremely stable. Through the noted earnings reports and some up-and-down price movement, you can see how the stocks PE has remained relatively flat, suggesting a fair valuation. I think, based on the price/book also being below the industry, that FSLR is slightly undervalued. Also, assuming they come in with a strong EPS as analysts predict, I think in a week from now, FSLR will be appealing at an even further discount.

Track the Balance Sheet

First Solar’s balance sheet remains financially strong and conservatively positioned, but some key metrics show moderate changes over time. The company continues to hold a substantial cash and short‑term investment base of over $2 billion, which exceeds its reported total debt of around $890 million, leaving it in a net cash position, supporting liquidity, operational flexibility, and room for further capital expenditure.

Debt to equity has crept higher over the last few years to support capacity expansion and manufacturing buildouts but is still quite low among peers and is still under 10%. Similarly, their quick ratio has diminished during this expansionary period, now down to 1.38, but it still reflects liquid assets meaningfully exceeding short‑term liabilities.

I would consider the dampening balance sheet a small risk to this stock, but even that is hard to justify. Cash position and assets are still in a solid position, and I won’t be concerned unless this starts to flip negative or debt to equity rises more toward 30% in upcoming quarters.

Expansion & Demand

FSLR continues to strategically expand its manufacturing footprint to meet rapidly growing global solar demand. The company is adding a fifth U.S. factory with approximately 3.7 GW of annual module capacity, expected to begin production in late 2026. They have had recent growth already in Alabama and Louisiana and are capturing policy incentives and tariff avoidance from greater domestic manufacturing. They currently do not manufacture in China, which has become a strategic advantage amongst competitors paying high tariffs, which has led to their nice profitability. The unique sustainability profile and performance characteristics of thin‑film technology that FSLR has pairs nicely with the demand that is being driven by corporate and government renewable energy targets and grid decarbonization goals.

Conclusion

FSLR has corrected just in time for what I suspect will be solid Q4 earnings. The stock is already valued fair to discounted, which could look cheaper with a strong report. Their balance sheet has been a little rocky, but they are still in a great cash position and have strong margins to fall back on. Higher module sales will continue on higher demand, and their already efficient production looks like it could continue to strengthen through vertical integration and expansion in domestic capacity. Buy this dip from FSLR.

data.title: Silvercorp Metals: What Silver Price Is Embedded In Today's $2.43B Valuation?
data.description:
December Baseline

In December, I valued Silvercorp Metals Inc. (SVM) at approximately $1.374 billion using a conservative sum-of-the-parts approach. At that time, SVM traded at $7.83, market cap was ~$1.59 billion, and my take was a neutral Hold.

My thesis was simple: China was producing solid cash flow, El Domo was still under construction, so I discounted it by 45%, and its market valuation left no margin of safety.

Today, however, SVM share price is $10.98, up 40%; market cap is $2.43 billion, Enterprise value (EV) is $2.24 billion - and management clearly says that the upside came from aggressively higher silver price then, which again experienced an extreme spike to $118.70 in January before retracing toward ~$77.

The key question is not where silver traded at its January peak, but what sustained silver price regime would justify a $2.43 billion valuation on a forward basis.

Q3 Operating Inputs

From their Q3 2026 results announced last week, SVM’s revenue was $126.11 million, up 50.8% year over year. After-smelter realized silver prices were $49.0/oz, whereas AISC (net of by-products) was $12.86/oz:

All-in sustaining cost per ounce of silver ("AISC") (net of by-product credits): $12.86, remaining flat with $12.75 in Q3 Fiscal 2025;

72% of the quarter’s revenue came from silver, while other associated metals like gold etc produced the rest. However, management has clearly stated that performance was mainly driven by an 80% hike in silver prices [this was in Q3: silver spot prices reached an unprecedented ~$118.70 in January, to fall back ~30% to $77 today. I have carefully used the Q3 framework all through].

Also note that their quarterly silver production was ~1.9 million oz, which is ~7.6 million oz annualized.

Isolate China Operating Value

SVM’s current EV is ~$2.24 billion, which already reflects the company’s net cash position because EV = Market Cap – Net Cash (if net cash positive). Thus, the $2.24 billion figure represents the market value of operating assets after accounting for balance sheet cash.

Using that value, let us try to figure out how much China represented in operating value. For that, subtract non-China components, so the ~$245 million equity portfolio, El Domo, assuming invested capital only, whose construction budget has now increased to $284 million, and marginal amounts from the Kuanping expansion. Let us conservatively assign a combined value of ~$500 million. Therefore, China represents an EV of $2.24B − 0.50B ≈ $1.74B.

This is the value the market assigns to the China silver business of SVM.

Implied Sustainable China Cash Flow

Now, we will assume a mid-cycle multiple of 5.5x operating cash flow. That gives us an implied sustainable China OCF of:-

1.74B ÷ 5.5 ≈ $316M annually (using silver-dominant economics for simplification)

Let us take a range of $300–320 million, which is the cash flow embedded in the current valuation.

Convert Implied OCF to Silver Price

In order to convert this implied OCF into a measurable silver price value, let us take:

  • Annual silver production = 7.6M oz

  • AISC = $12.86/oz

  • X = sustained realized silver price

Therefore, silver margin per oz = X − 12.86

Annual silver margin:

7.6M × (X − 12.86)

If we equate this to $300 million, the lower value of our earlier OCF range, we get:

7.6M × (X − 12.86) = 300M

Solving for X, we have:

X − 12.86 = 39.5X ≈ $52.36 per ounce

That means the current valuation implies sustained realized silver in the low $50s.

Observe that even with spot silver currently around ~$77 following its $118.70 January spike, SVM’s present valuation still back-solves to a low-$50s sustained regime. That suggests the equity market itself is treating recent spot levels as transitory rather than structural. If the market believed $77 silver was durable, SVM would mathematically imply a materially higher embedded silver price than ~$52–53.

Sensitivity Analysis

Given the inputs we previously had, assumed constant here, we will apply three dynamic multiples to account for bull/bear scenarios. So, we will take 4.5x for a down-cycle compression scenario, 5.5x as the current cycle implied multiple, and 6.0x for a bull scenario.

This gives us the following sensitivity table:

Now, let us convert this back to equity value. So, we will add back non-China assets (~$500M) to China EV. This will give us various values. To take one example, at $53 silver, 5.5x multiple, we have $1.70B as China EV, and adding back the $0.50B non-China assets, we get $2.20B as implied EV. This is close to the current EV of $2.24B, which is why we derived embedded silver at around $52-53.

Now, how do we interpret this table? For the downsize case, look at the second row, with silver at $45, and a compressed 4.5x multiple. Here, China EV is $1.05B, adding non-China assets gives us an implied EV of $1.55. Versus current EV of $2.24B, this implies a ~30% EV downside.

Take the mid-case, with silver at $50 and 5.5x multiple. Here, China EV is $1.55B, so implied EV is $2.05B, slightly below current EV.

The embedded case, with silver at $53 and a 5.5x multiple, gives us fair value. This shows mild optimism, given the actual silver value was $49 at the time.

Now take a normalized high case where silver stabilizes around $60 — materially below the January spike of $118.70 but above prior mid-cycle norms - and apply a bullish 6x multiple. That gives us a China EV of $2.2B and an implied EV of $2.7B. This represents a 15-20% upside from current SVM levels.

The simple mathematical conclusion we can draw here is that downside convexity is higher than upside convexity. In other words, despite spot silver near ~$77, the market valuation still implies a sustained low-$50s regime. That leaves very little upside if silver rises further, but a much larger downside if it goes down. Therefore, if you are betting on SVM at these prices, you are effectively betting on silver sustaining elevated levels after one of the most volatile rallies in the past decade.

Conclusion: Hold

As the math here shows, meaningful upside requires silver to stabilize durably at elevated levels rather than reverting toward the $45–55 range implied by the current valuation framework.

Now, the 2025 silver rally came from a combination of gold momentum spillover. There was also higher industrial demand, mainly solar. There has been limited growth in primary mines in recent years, which constrained supply and helped drive up prices. Finally, there was a high volume of speculation in the market.

However, silver exhibits higher mean reversion than gold, so it often goes down once marginal demand falls. Its industrial usage makes it more price-sensitive than gold. Mines also tend to produce more as demand increases, taking prices down eventually. Thus, while silver can spike well above historical norms on momentum - as evidenced by the recent move above $100 - the probability of it stabilizing at such extremes remains lower than a consolidation in the $45-55 range.

That being the case, our valuation should be anchored in a more conservative base case. Unless the investor has a strong conviction that silver can remain structurally elevated well above prior mid-cycle norms, SVM offers limited upside relative to downside risk under mean reversion. Thus, the appropriate stance at $10.98 is Hold.
